anchor.snippet: A cellular automaton consists of a regular grid of cells
a set of cells called its neighborhood is defined relative to the specified cell
An initial state (time t = 0) is selected by assigning a state for each cell
The primary classifications of cellular automata, as outlined by Wolfram, are numbered one to four
Special types of cellular automata are reversible , where only a single configuration leads directly to a subsequent one
totalistic , in which the future value of individual cells only depends on the total value of a group of neighboring cells
The two most common types of neighborhoods are the von Neumann neighborhood and the Moore neighborhood
Another common neighborhood type is the extended von Neumann neighborhood , which includes the two closest cells in each orthogonal direction
The general equation for the total number of automata possible is
Thus, in the two-dimensional system with a Moore neighborhood, the total number of automata possible would be
the assignment of state values is called a configuration
These cells are usually handled with periodic boundary conditions resulting in a toroidal arrangement
in a 1-dimensional cellular automaton like the examples below, the neighborhood of a cell
The most fundamental result is the characterization in the Curtis–Hedlund–Lyndon theorem
He proved that two-dimensional CA are computation universal, introduced 1-dimensional CA, and showed that they too are computation universal
its rules are as follows: Any live cell with fewer than two live neighbours dies
after much effort it has been shown that the Game of Life can emulate a universal Turing machine
suggested that Rule 110 may be universal —a fact proved later by Wolfram's research assistant Matthew Cook
Class 1: Nearly all initial patterns evolve quickly into a stable, homogeneous state
Class 2: Nearly all initial patterns evolve quickly into stable or oscillating structures
Class 3: Nearly all initial patterns evolve in a pseudo-random or chaotic manner
Class 4: Nearly all initial patterns evolve into structures that interact in complex and interesting ways
Wolfram has conjectured that many class 4 cellular automata, if not all, are capable of universal computation
Culik and Yu proposed three well-defined classes (and a fourth one for the automata not matching any of these)
membership in these proved undecidable
A cellular automaton is reversible if, for every current configuration of the cellular automaton, there is exactly one past configuration
reversibility implies that this function is bijective

annotations.label: Time-reversed reversible CA is a CA

anchor.snippet: If a cellular automaton is reversible, its time-reversed behavior can also be described as a cellular automaton
the configurations without preimages are called Garden of Eden patterns
for cellular automata of two or more dimensions reversibility is undecidable
every reversible cellular automaton can be emulated by a block cellular automaton
The state of each cell in a totalistic cellular automaton is represented by a number
If the state of the cell at time t depends on both its own state and the total of its neighbors at time t − 1 then the cellular automaton is properly called outer totalistic
Outer totalistic cellular automata with the same Moore neighborhood structure as Life are sometimes called life-like cellular automata
an isotropic ruleset is one that is not necessarily outer totalistic, but still has all the reflection symmetries
Such cellular automata are called probabilistic cellular automata
There are continuous automata
Continuous spatial automata have a continuum of locations
Graph rewriting automata are extensions of cellular automata based on graph rewriting systems
The simplest nontrivial cellular automaton would be one-dimensional, with two possible states per cell
There are then 2 8 = 256 possible rules
These 256 cellular automata are generally referred to by their Wolfram code
Rule 30 exhibits class 3 behavior, meaning even simple input patterns such as that shown lead to chaotic, seemingly random histories
Rule 110, like the Game of Life, exhibits what Wolfram calls class 4 behavior
all elementary cellular automaton rules can be considered to sit on the vertices of the 8-dimensional unit hypercube
This rule-to-rule distance is also called the Hamming distance
it is shown that class 4 rules are located between the class 1 and class 3 rules
the widespread species Conus textile bears a pattern resembling Wolfram's rule 30 cellular automaton
The Ising model is a prototypical example, in which each cell can be in either of two states called "up" and "down"
Two well-known such cellular automata, Maze and Mazectric, have rulestrings B3/S12345 and B3/S1234
